preamble: The shift from the voiceless bilabial stop [p] to its voiced counterpart [b] is a classic example of voicing lenition, exemplified in both Romance and Uralic historical phonology. In Romance, it occurs in intervocalic positions where the stop adopts the voicing of surrounding vowels. In Uralic (esp. Finnish), it represents the weak grade of consonant gradation, a grammaticalized alternation affecting stops following stressed vowels in closed syllables.

examples.note: Latin intervocalic [p] voices to [b] in Spanish.
Latin [p] before [r] voices to [b] in Spanish.
French historical voicing [p] > [b], then further lenition to [v].
Intervocalic voicing: single voiceless stops are realized as voiced when between vowels (e.g., /p/ → [b]).
Consonant gradation weak grade: word-medial /p/ voices to [b] in closed syllable context.
Systematic intervocalic voicing in weak grade: /p/ → [b] when in weak-grade position of the consonant gradation alternation.
Intervocalic voicing: /p/ is realized as [b] between voiced sounds (e.g., 'bibimbap' [pibimdap]).

sources: Penny: A History of the Spanish Language
